policy_refs: [CS-RULE-001, CS-RULE-002, CS-RULE-003, CS-RULE-004, CS-RULE-009, CS-RULE-010, CS-RULE-011, CS-RULE-018, CS-RULE-019, CS-RULE-020, CS-RULE-021]
kb_refs: [Section 2.2 - Router & WiFi Facts, Section 3.1 - Billing Concepts]
expected_outcome: Customer raises TWO unrelated issues in one call: (1) intermittent connection drops every evening, and (2) a $7 line-item on his bill he doesn't recognize. Marcus handles both cleanly: diagnoses the drops as 2.4GHz channel congestion and queues a firmware push + channel change (SOP-03), then explains the $7 charge is a regional sports surcharge he opted into and removes it going forward (SOP-02). Re-engages during the line scan (CS-RULE-009). PASS — exemplary multi-issue handling, both threads resolved and summarized separately.

coverage.notes: CS-RULE-003 — PASS (captures both issues up front)

coverage.element: A.C.E.S. light on the surprise charge

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Thank you for calling NexaLink Telecommunications Broadband and Support. My name is Marcus. Who do I have the pleasure of speaking with today?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: And this call may be recorded for quality assurance and training purposes.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Hi Marcus, Nathan Brooks. I've actually got two separate things — my internet keeps dropping in the evenings, and there's a charge on my bill I don't recognize. Figured I'd do both in one call.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Smart — let's knock out both. I appreciate you batching them.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: So I've got two items: an intermittent connection drop in the evenings, and a billing line you don't recognize. I'll take the connection issue first since that's usually the bigger headache, then we'll sort the bill. Before I pull anything up, let me verify the account — your account number?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Six-six-nine-zero, two-two-one-eight, seven-seven.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Thank you. Last four of the payment method on file?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Three-three-zero-one.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: And the full name on the account?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Nathan James Brooks.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Perfect, you're fully verified, Mr. Brooks.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Great.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Let's start with the drops. When you say evenings — roughly what time, and does everything drop at once or just some devices?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Around eight, nine PM. It's everything on WiFi — phones, the TV. The drop lasts a minute or two then comes back. Wired stuff seems fine.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: That last detail is the key one — wired is fine, only WiFi drops, and it clusters in the evening. That pattern is almost always channel congestion on the 2.4 gigahertz band, when all your neighbours' networks get busy at the same time.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Huh. So it's not my equipment failing?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Doesn't look like it. The 2.4 band has only a few non-overlapping channels, and in dense areas they get crowded at peak hours. Two things fix it: pushing your router to auto-select a cleaner channel, and nudging your newer devices onto the 5 gigahertz band, which is far less congested. Let me run a quick line scan from my side first to confirm signal levels are healthy.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Go for it.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Starting the scan now.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: How long's it take?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: About thirty seconds. I'm still here with you, Mr. Brooks — the scan is running. Thank you for your patience.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: No problem.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Still going on my side — just gathering the channel data for your area.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Okay, scan's done. Your downstream signal and noise levels are clean — confirms the line itself is healthy. And sure enough, your 2.4 band is sitting on channel six, which in your area is the single most congested channel.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: That tracks. Channel six, of course.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: I'm going to do two things. First, I'll push a firmware update tonight that switches your router to automatic channel selection, so it'll move off congested channels on its own. Second, I'd suggest renaming your 5 gigahertz network to something obvious so you can connect your phone and TV to it directly — that band rarely congests.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Can you push the firmware now or does it have to be tonight?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: It queues for tonight around 3 AM so it doesn't interrupt you — the router reboots once during the push. By tomorrow morning you'll be on auto channel selection. If the evening drops continue past a couple of days, call back and we'll look at a band-steering config or a mesh node.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Great. That's issue one. Want to do the bill?

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Let's do it. What's the line item that caught your eye?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: There's a seven-dollar charge labelled R-S-N surcharge. I have no idea what that is.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Let me pull the bill. R-S-N stands for Regional Sports Network — it's a surcharge that comes bundled when a TV package includes regional sports channels.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: I can see how that's confusing if you didn't knowingly sign up for it. Let me check how it got on there before we assume anything.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Please.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Okay — it was added when you took the Sports Plus add-on back in March. It's a real opt-in on the account, not an error, but it is optional. If you're not watching the regional sports channels, we can remove the add-on and the seven-dollar surcharge goes with it.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Honestly I forgot I even added that. I don't watch it. Take it off.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Removing Sports Plus and the R-S-N surcharge now. That's a seven-dollar reduction starting your next billing cycle.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Good. I'd rather keep the seven bucks.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Done — it's off. Just so it's clear: this isn't a refund of a wrongful charge, since it was a real opt-in. It's a removal going forward. From next cycle, it's gone.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: That's fair. I did have it, I just didn't use it. Going forward is fine.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Exactly right. So to give you the full picture on both — the evening drops are channel congestion on 2.4 gigahertz, not a fault, and the surcharge was a real but forgotten add-on.

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Makes sense on both counts.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: To summarize the two items: One — for the evening WiFi drops, I confirmed the line is healthy, queued a firmware push tonight to enable automatic channel selection, and recommended moving your devices to the 5 gigahertz band. Two — for the bill, I removed the Sports Plus add-on and its seven-dollar regional sports surcharge effective next cycle. Does that all sound right?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Perfect. Two for two. Thanks, Marcus.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: My pleasure. Is there anything else I can help you with today, Mr. Brooks?

turns.speaker: CUSTOMER
turns.text: Nope, you covered it.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Your case reference number is N-X-L dash three-eight-zero-six-six-two-one. You'll receive a brief survey after we hang up — if you have a moment, we'd really appreciate the feedback.

turns.speaker: AGENT
turns.text: Thank you for being a NexaLink customer, Mr. Brooks. Have a great rest of your evening.
